name: Tharsis, Gibraleón – return via Trigueros
description: This difficult 70.7-mile (113.8 km) road cycling route in Huelva province offers a challenging ride with mining heritage views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Alosno traverse an undulating landscape of rolling hills and extensive plains within the El Andévalo region of Huelva, Andalusia. The area is characterized by a mix of agricultural lands, cork oak and holm oak forests, and a dense network of ravines and watercourses like the River Oraque. Cyclists encounter varied terrain, including historical mining landscapes with abandoned open-pit mines, adding a unique visual dimension to the natural environment. This region offers diverse road cycling opportunities across its green countryside. Best road cycling routes around Alosno The most popular road cycling route is Tharsis, Gibraleón – return via Trigueros, a 70.7 miles (113.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 54 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,340 feet (1018 meters) of elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Villanueva de los Castillejos, Puebla de Guzmán, Tharsis, San Bartolomé de la Torre – circular route, a moderate 41.3 miles (66.5 km) path. This route offers varied scenery through several towns and rural landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the Tharsis, Puebla de Guzmán, Cabezas Rubias – circular route, a 52.0 miles (83.8 km) trail leading through agricultural lands and historical mining areas, often completed in about 3 hours 41 minutes. Road cycling around Alosno is defined by rolling hills, extensive plains, and historical mining landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 250 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alosno's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Alosno?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes around Alosno, offering a variety of experiences for cyclists. These routes range from moderate to difficult, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Alosno?
A: Road cycling routes around Alosno feature an undulating landscape of rolling hills and extensive plains. You'll encounter a mix of agricultural lands, cork oak and holm oak forests, and a dense network of ravines and watercourses. The terrain provides both climbs and descents, making for varied and engaging rides.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of road cycling routes in Alosno?
A: The road cycling routes in Alosno are categorized as moderate to difficult. There are 17 moderate routes and 8 difficult routes, ensuring options for cyclists looking for both challenging climbs and scenic, less strenuous rides.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Alosno area?
A: Yes, several popular routes around Alosno are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991342727/villanueva-de-los-castillejos-puebla-de-guzman-tharsis-san-bartolome-de-la-torre-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de los Castillejos, Puebla de Guzmán, Tharsis, San Bartolomé de la Torre – circular route&lt;/a&gt; offers varied scenery, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991481627/tharsis-puebla-de-guzman-cabezas-rubias-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tharsis, Puebla de Guzmán, Cabezas Rubias – circular route&lt;/a&gt; takes you through agricultural lands and historical mining areas.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling near Alosno?
A: While cycling, you can explore the unique historical mining landscapes with abandoned open-pit mines like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5334987" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tharsis Open-Pit Mine&lt;/a&gt;, which adds a distinct visual dimension. The region also features green valleys, extensive plains, and the picturesque River Oraque. You might also find quiet country roads, perfect for peaceful rides.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Alosno?
A: The westernmost Huelva province, including Alosno, benefits from Atlantic winds, resulting in greener landscapes. Spring offers lush, multi-colored blooms, and summers are generally cooler and more verdant than other parts of Andalusia, making these seasons particularly pleasant for cycling.

Q: Are there any particularly long or challenging road cycling routes in Alosno?
A: For a challenging ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991334051/tharsis-gibraleon-return-via-trigueros" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tharsis, Gibraleón – return via Trigueros&lt;/a&gt; route. This difficult trail spans over 70 miles (113 km) and features more than 3,340 feet (1018 meters) of elevation gain, typically taking nearly 5 hours to complete.

Q: What cultural or historical points of interest are near the cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, Alosno itself is known as the 'Cuna del Fandango' (Cradle of Fandango) and features historic architecture like the 18th-century Parroquia de Nuestra Señora Gracia. The well-preserved 19th-century Medio Millar railway station, part of the historic Tharsis Railway, is also noteworthy and can be found along some routes.

Q: Can I find routes that offer elevated views or viewpoints?
A: The undulating landscape of Alosno, with its rolling hills, naturally provides elevated sections that offer scenic views of the surrounding countryside. While specific viewpoints are not detailed for every route, the varied terrain ensures many opportunities for panoramic vistas, especially in areas like Cabezo Juré, known for its elevated positions.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on road cycling routes around Alosno?
A: The elevation gain on routes around Alosno varies significantly with difficulty. Moderate routes might see around 600-750 meters (2,000-2,500 feet) of ascent, while more difficult routes, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991334051/tharsis-gibraleon-return-via-trigueros" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tharsis, Gibraleón – return via Trigueros&lt;/a&gt;, can feature over 1,000 meters (3,300 feet) of elevation gain.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through multiple towns or villages?
A: Yes, some routes are designed to connect several local towns and villages, offering a glimpse into the regional culture and architecture. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991342727/villanueva-de-los-castillejos-puebla-de-guzman-tharsis-san-bartolome-de-la-torre-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de los Castillejos, Puebla de Guzmán, Tharsis, San Bartolomé de la Torre – circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a great example, providing varied scenery as you cycle through different communities.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Alosno?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the varied terrain, which includes rolling hills and extensive plains, and the unique visual dimension added by historical mining landscapes. The green countryside and quiet roads are also often highlighted for providing a peaceful and engaging cycling experience.
tips: This village has been a mining area for many centuries, in whose mines, above all, massive pyrite was extracted for the manufacture of sulfuric acid.

A beautiful town located in Tierra Llana, near the capital and located on the banks of the Odiel River, on land dedicated to vine, olive and legume agriculture. Within its term is part of the Natural Area of the Odiel Marshes, a very interesting wetland, both for its wealth of birds and plants. In the town center you have to see the Castle and the Parish Church.

Its main productive activity is the cultivation of cereals, sunflowers, olive trees, citrus fruits, legumes, and the breeding of sheep, pigs and wild cattle. Its important monuments include the Dolmen de Soto, the Church of San Antón, the Convent of Carmen, the Jesuit College and the Hermitage of Santa Misericordia.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for road cycling, with approximately 82.1 km of asphalt and 1.40 km of paved surfaces. However, be aware that some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride, requiring good bike handling skills and potentially wider tires than typical racebike setups. The overall elevation gain is around 1,020 meters, making it a challenging ride.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires 'good fitness' due to its significant length of approximately 114 kilometers and over 1,000 meters of elevation gain. It's best suited for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What points of interest can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through areas with historical significance. You'll encounter the Tharsis Open-Pit Mine, which highlights the region's mining heritage. You'll also pass through the Gibraleón Town Center and near the ancient Dolmen de Soto, a notable historical site.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the Huelva province of Andalusia, known for its diverse landscapes. While specific scenic details for this exact route are not extensively detailed, the broader region offers a unique combination of coastal landscapes, rich history, and lush nature. You can expect views influenced by the mining heritage around Tharsis and the general rural beauty of the area.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, starting points like Tharsis or Gibraleón would be logical places to look for public parking. It's advisable to check local parking regulations and availability in these towns before your ride.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for completing this 114-kilometer racebike route is approximately 4 hours and 54 minutes (17,657 seconds). This is an estimate for continuous riding and does not include stops for breaks or sightseeing.

faqs.question: Is this a loop route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is described as 'Tharsis, Gibraleón – return via Trigueros,' indicating it is a loop that starts and ends in the same general area, passing through these towns.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this route?
faqs.answer: The Huelva province generally experiences mild winters and hot summers. For comfortable road cycling, spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are typically the best times, offering pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not suitable for cycling with pets due to the speed and distance involved. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier, and always adhere to local leash laws in towns.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route, as it primarily uses public roads and passes through towns. However, always respect private property and local regulations.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this ride?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and difficulty, you should bring plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Due to potential unpaved segments, consider tires that can handle varied surfaces.

faqs.question: Are there places to refuel or find amenities along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through towns like Gibraleón and Trigueros, you will find opportunities for cafes, shops, and other amenities to refuel and rest.
